threats.title: SurplusGuildMinter.getRewards() Checks Slashed Flag Against Uninitialized Memory, Enabling Permissionless Stake Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: In SurplusGuildMinter.getRewards(), the `slashed` boolean is determined by comparing `lastGaugeLoss` against `userStake.lastGaugeLoss` BEFORE `userStake` is loaded from storage. Since `userStake` is a return variable initialized to zero, `userStake.lastGaugeLoss` is always 0 at the comparison point. Any term with a historical gauge loss (lastGaugeLoss > 0) causes ALL stakers on that term to be incorrectly marked as slashed. The getRewards function then zeroes out the user's stake, permanently locking their CREDIT in the term surplus buffer. Anyone can call getRewards(victim, term) to trigger this.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code bug where variable ordering causes a logic error. The comparison at line ~246 happens before the storage load at line ~251. Any on-chain user can exploit this permissionlessly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: slashed = (lastGaugeLoss > uint256(userStake.lastGaugeLoss)) evaluated when userStake is default-initialized memory (all zeros), before userStake = _stakes[user][term] loads actual data

preconditions: Target term has had at least one gauge loss (lastGaugeLoss > 0)
Victim user has an active stake on that term (stakeTime != 0)
No special roles required - getRewards is public and callable by anyone

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Return variable `userStake` is declared as return parameter (default zero), used in comparison at line ~246, then overwritten from storage at line ~251
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent for any term that has ever had a loss event

operational_questions: How many terms have historical gauge losses?
What is the total CREDIT staked via SurplusGuildMinter on affected terms?
Can slashed users' CREDIT in termSurplusBuffer ever be recovered?
validation_requirements: Deploy a test where a term has a loss, then a user stakes, then a third party calls getRewards - confirm stake is zeroed
Verify that unstake() returns early for incorrectly-slashed users, permanently locking their CREDIT
Confirm that the GUILD gauge weight is not decremented when internal slash occurs

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify any LendingTerm with lastGaugeLoss > 0 (any past bad debt event)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Enumerate all SurplusGuildMinter stakers on that term via event logs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call getRewards(victimAddress, termAddress) for each staker - their stake is zeroed, CREDIT locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No profit for attacker directly, but grief destroys first-loss capital and GUILD gauge weight consistency
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - single transaction per victim, no capital needed

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: All SurplusGuildMinter positions on any term with historical loss are vulnerable
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: First-loss capital permanently locked in surplus buffers; GUILD gauge weights become inconsistent (ghost weight)
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires governor emergencyAction to rescue funds from ProfitManager surplus buffers
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: SurplusGuildMinter staking/unstaking, gauge weight accuracy, surplus buffer accounting

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Anytime after a term experiences any loss

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor getRewards() calls that emit Unstake events for users who didn't initiate
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: termSurplusBuffer growing without corresponding active stakes
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Mass getRewards calls from unknown addresses across affected terms

threats.title: LendingTermOffboarding canOffboard Flag Persists Across Re-onboarding, Allowing Immediate Permissionless Offboarding Without New Poll
threats.hypothesis_description: When a term is offboarded via LendingTermOffboarding, canOffboard[term] is set to true. This flag is only reset to false in cleanup(). If governance re-onboards the term (via LendingTermOnboarding) without first calling cleanup() (e.g., because loans are still open), canOffboard[term] remains true. Any user can then call offboard(term) immediately after re-onboarding, bypassing the entire democratic polling process. The removeGauge call in offboard() will succeed because re-onboarding removes the term from _deprecatedGauges.
threats.investigation_rationale: The canOffboard flag has no lifecycle coupling with the gauge state. Re-onboarding a term doesn't check or reset this flag, creating a stale authorization that enables permissionless re-offboarding.

proof_of_signal.pattern: offboard() only checks canOffboard[term] which persists from prior cycle; cleanup() is the only function that sets canOffboard to false; no reset on re-onboarding

preconditions: A term was previously offboarded (canOffboard = true)
Governance re-onboards the term without calling cleanup() first
The LendingTermOffboarding contract is not paused

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: canOffboard is a persistent boolean with no linkage to the onboarding/offboarding cycle number or gauge state
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Governance must have re-onboarded a previously offboarded term - this is a documented legitimate action
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent after re-onboarding until cleanup is called on the prior offboarding

operational_questions: Can cleanup() be called before re-onboarding (requires issuance == 0)?
Is there a race condition between re-onboarding timelock execution and attacker's offboard call?
Does this create cascading issues with nOffboardingsInProgress?
validation_requirements: Test sequence: offboard term -> re-onboard term -> call offboard(term) without new poll
Verify nOffboardingsInProgress counter state after double offboard
Check if PSM redemptions become permanently paused

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for terms being re-onboarded that have canOffboard == true
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for the re-onboarding timelock action to execute (addGauge succeeds)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Immediately call offboard(term) - no poll or quorum needed
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Term is immediately deprecated again, disrupting users who voted for it

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Re-onboarded term immediately deprecated; GUILD voters' allocations disrupted; PSM redemptions may be paused
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Governance must re-onboard again, but attacker can repeat indefinitely
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Term onboarding lifecycle, PSM redemptions, GUILD voting

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: offboard() called on a term that was just re-onboarded
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: offboard() calls from addresses that didn't participate in any poll

threats.title: nOffboardingsInProgress Counter Corruption via Re-onboard/Re-offboard Permanently Pauses PSM Redemptions
threats.hypothesis_description: Building on OPS-H002, when a term is offboarded twice (once before re-onboarding, once after), nOffboardingsInProgress is incremented twice. However, cleanup() can only be called once per offboard cycle (it sets canOffboard to false). After cleanup decrements the counter once, the residual +1 remains permanently. Since PSM redemptions are paused when nOffboardingsInProgress > 0 and unpaused only when it reaches 0, the permanent residual keeps redemptions paused forever. This effectively bricks the PSM for all CREDIT holders.
threats.investigation_rationale: The nOffboardingsInProgress counter is the sole mechanism controlling PSM redemption pausing. A double-increment with single-decrement creates an irrecoverable state without governor emergencyAction intervention.

proof_of_signal.pattern: offboard() increments nOffboardingsInProgress unconditionally; cleanup() decrements it and sets canOffboard=false; second offboard after re-onboard increments again but cleanup can never fire a second time for same canOffboard flag

preconditions: OPS-H002 conditions are met (term offboarded, re-onboarded without cleanup)
Attacker can call offboard(term) after re-onboarding
PSM is not already permanently paused by another mechanism

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: nOffboardingsInProgress has no upper bound check and no mechanism to correct miscounts
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Recovery requires governor emergencyAction to directly manipulate SimplePSM.setRedemptionsPaused(false) or LendingTermOffboarding state
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After re-onboarding of a previously offboarded term

operational_questions: Can governor's setRedemptionsPaused(false) on SimplePSM override the offboarding-driven pause?
What is the economic impact of permanently paused PSM redemptions?
Can nOffboardingsInProgress underflow if cleanup is called without matching offboard?
validation_requirements: Simulate: offboard -> re-onboard -> offboard -> cleanup -> verify nOffboardingsInProgress > 0
Verify PSM.redemptionsPaused remains true after cleanup
Test if governor can manually unpause PSM independent of nOffboardingsInProgress

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Execute OPS-H002 to offboard a re-onboarded term
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: nOffboardingsInProgress is now 2 (incremented at first offboard and second offboard)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for cleanup to be called (requires issuance == 0) - counter decrements to 1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: PSM redemptions remain paused permanently - CREDIT holders cannot redeem for peg tokens

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: PSM peg stability mechanism permanently disabled
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All CREDIT holders unable to redeem for peg tokens; loss of CREDIT peg; potential bank run
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires governor emergencyAction - may take days/weeks through timelock
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: SimplePSM.redeem, CREDIT token peg, protocol solvency perception

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: nOffboardingsInProgress value diverging from actual deprecated terms awaiting cleanup
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: PSM redemptions paused with no legitimate offboarding in progress
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rapid offboard/re-onboard cycles on a single term

threats.title: GuildVetoGovernor Veto Grief: First-Mover Creates Veto Early to Prevent Quorum, Blocking Future Veto Attempts on Same Timelock Action
threats.hypothesis_description: GuildVetoGovernor.createVeto() generates a deterministic proposalId from the timelockId (via hashProposal of fixed targets/values/calldatas/description). Once a veto proposal is created, OZ Governor prevents creating another with the same proposalId ('Governor: proposal already exists'). The voting snapshot is taken at the block of createVeto(). An attacker can front-run legitimate veto attempts by calling createVeto() immediately after a timelock action is queued, locking the snapshot at a moment when insufficient voting power is delegated. If the veto fails to reach quorum, no second attempt is possible for that timelock action.
threats.investigation_rationale: createVeto is permissionless and the proposalId is deterministic. The snapshot-based voting power is locked at proposal creation, not at a governance-chosen block.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Deterministic proposalId via super.propose() with fixed description from timelockId; OZ Governor rejects duplicate proposalIds

preconditions: A malicious or controversial timelock action is queued
Voting power at the moment of createVeto() is low (e.g., tokens undelegated)
Quorum cannot be reached with the snapshot's voting power distribution

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: createVeto() is fully permissionless with no proposal threshold; anyone can snapshot voting power at any moment
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From when a timelock action is queued until the first createVeto call

operational_questions: What is the typical delegation pattern - is voting power usually delegated or undelegated?
How quickly do token holders delegate after a controversial proposal appears?
Can the veto governor be paired with a proposal threshold to prevent griefing?
validation_requirements: Create a veto when few tokens are delegated, verify veto fails
Attempt to create a second veto for the same timelockId, confirm revert
Verify the malicious timelock action executes without possibility of veto

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Immediately call createVeto(timelockId) when voting power delegation is low
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Veto proposal snapshot captures low delegation state
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Quorum unreachable - malicious action executes without community veto power

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Veto governance mechanism completely neutralized for target action
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Malicious governance actions cannot be vetoed by token holders
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery - timelock action executes as scheduled
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All timelock-gated governance actions subject to veto

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - need to monitor timelock and understand voting dynamics
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Immediately after timelock action is queued, when delegation is minimal

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: createVeto calls from addresses with no voting power
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Veto proposals created immediately after timelock queuing

threats.title: Rebase Distribution Delay Attack: Frequent Small distribute() Calls Perpetually Delay Reward Materialization for All CREDIT Rebasing Holders
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20RebaseDistributor.distribute() resets the interpolation period for BOTH the share price AND unminted rebase rewards to a new 30-day window from the current timestamp. Each call overwrites __rebasingSharePrice.lastTimestamp and targetTimestamp, and __unmintedRebaseRewards similarly. An attacker can call distribute(1) repeatedly (burning just 1 wei of CREDIT each time) to continuously reset the interpolation, ensuring that accumulated rewards from legitimate distributions are never fully materialized to rebasing holders within a reasonable timeframe.
threats.investigation_rationale: The distribute function has no minimum amount check beyond != 0, and each call resets ALL pending interpolation timers, not just the new distribution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Each distribute() overwrites __rebasingSharePrice with lastTimestamp=block.timestamp and targetTimestamp=block.timestamp+DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD; similarly for __unmintedRebaseRewards; no accumulation of prior incomplete interpolations

preconditions: Attacker holds at least 1 wei of CREDIT (cost is negligible)
rebasingSupply > 0 (at least one account is rebasing)
Attacker calls distribute(1) frequently (e.g., every block or daily)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: distribute(uint256) has no minimum amount - distribute(1) is valid
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Continuous - attacker must keep calling distribute(1) to maintain delay

operational_questions: How much CREDIT does the attacker need to burn to sustain the attack over time?
Does the newTargetSharePrice calculation correctly handle very small amounts?
What is the actual delay impact on reward materialization per distribution frequency?
validation_requirements: Simulate legitimate distribution of 1000 CREDIT followed by repeated distribute(1) calls
Measure actual share price interpolation progress vs expected
Calculate attacker cost vs victim impact ratio

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Acquire minimal CREDIT (even 1 wei per call)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call distribute(1) every block or periodically
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All pending rebase rewards are perpetually delayed in materialization
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Rebasing CREDIT holders receive rewards much slower than intended 30-day schedule
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Automated bot to call distribute(1) regularly

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Savings rate effectively reduced to near-zero for rebasing holders
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Stops as soon as attacker stops calling distribute(1), but accumulated delay persists
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: CREDIT rebasing rewards, savings rate attractiveness

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Frequent distribute() calls with tiny amounts from same address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Share price interpolation never reaching target value

threats.title: Debt Ceiling Check in _decrementGaugeWeight Prevents Slashing of GUILD Voters on Fully-Utilized Terms
threats.hypothesis_description: GuildToken._decrementGaugeWeight() requires that after decrementing weight, the term's issuance does not exceed the new debt ceiling: `require(issuance <= debtCeilingAfterDecrement)`. This check also applies during applyGaugeLoss(), which calls _decrementGaugeWeight to remove the voter's weight. If a term's issuance is at or near its debt ceiling, reducing gauge weight would drop the ceiling below issuance, causing the require to revert. This prevents slashing of GUILD voters who should be penalized for a loss-generating term, defeating the core slashing mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The slashing mechanism's correctness depends on applyGaugeLoss being callable. The debt ceiling check creates a circular dependency: voters can't be slashed because their weight maintains the debt ceiling that prevents slashing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _decrementGaugeWeight checks LendingTerm(gauge).debtCeiling(-int256(weight)) against issuance; applyGaugeLoss calls _decrementGaugeWeight; if issuance >= debtCeilingAfterDecrement, revert blocks slashing

preconditions: A term has generated bad debt (loss notified via notifyPnL)
The term's issuance is at or near its current debt ceiling
Loans on the term haven't been fully repaid/called yet

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: applyGaugeLoss -> _decrementGaugeWeight -> debtCeiling check creates circular dependency where slashing requires reducing weight but reducing weight requires issuance below ceiling
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - affects core protocol slashing mechanism
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: While term has active loans near debt ceiling

operational_questions: After a term is deprecated (offboarded), does the debt ceiling check still apply?
Can loans be called first to reduce issuance before slashing?
Does this create a time-limited or permanent DoS on slashing?
validation_requirements: Test applyGaugeLoss on a term with issuance == debtCeiling
Verify that _decrementGaugeWeight reverts in this scenario
Test if offboarding (deprecating the gauge) changes the behavior

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: As a borrower on a term, maximize utilization by borrowing up to debt ceiling
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: As a voter for the same term (or colluding voter), benefit from high utilization
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If the term generates bad debt, the high utilization prevents applyGaugeLoss from succeeding
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Voters avoid slashing until issuance drops (loans are repaid/called/auctioned)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Borrower-voter coordination to maintain high utilization

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Core slashing mechanism (economic punishment for bad gauge votes) is defeated
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: GUILD voters face no consequences for backing loss-generating terms; moral hazard increases
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires calling all loans and completing auctions before slashing can proceed
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: GuildToken.applyGaugeLoss, transfer, burn for affected voters

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Capital to borrow near debt ceiling
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Before loss is applied, ensure term is at max utilization

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed applyGaugeLoss transactions; terms at max utilization after loss events
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Long delays between notifyGaugeLoss and applyGaugeLoss
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Borrowing activity increasing on a term right before/after loss notification

threats.title: ProfitManager.notifyPnL Reverts When Loss Exceeds CREDIT Total Supply After Surplus Buffer Depletion
threats.hypothesis_description: In ProfitManager.notifyPnL(), when processing a loss that exceeds the surplus buffer, the creditMultiplier update formula computes: `newCreditMultiplier = (creditMultiplier * (creditTotalSupply - loss)) / creditTotalSupply`. If `loss > creditTotalSupply` (after buffer depletion), the subtraction underflows and reverts. This can occur when creditMultiplier has been significantly reduced by prior losses, amplifying the principal of old loans (principal = borrowAmount * borrowCreditMultiplier / currentCreditMultiplier). The loss becomes unprocessable, blocking auction settlement and leaving the system in an inconsistent state.
threats.investigation_rationale: The formula has no floor protection. With sufficient prior markdown, a single defaulted loan's principal can mathematically exceed the entire CREDIT supply, making loss processing impossible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 newCreditMultiplier = (creditMultiplier * (creditTotalSupply - loss)) / creditTotalSupply; underflows if loss > creditTotalSupply

preconditions: creditMultiplier has been significantly reduced by prior losses (e.g., 0.1e18 or lower)
A loan opened at a higher creditMultiplier defaults
The amplified principal (borrowAmount * oldMultiplier / currentMultiplier) exceeds current total CREDIT supply minus surplus buffer
Requires specific sequence of losses and market conditions

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No bounds check on (creditTotalSupply - loss); principal amplification via creditMultiplier ratio can grow without bound
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - occurs during normal auction settlement flow
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After significant creditMultiplier reduction from prior bad debt

operational_questions: What creditMultiplier level makes this exploitable?
Can an attacker deliberately create cascading losses to reduce creditMultiplier?
What happens to the loan and auction when notifyPnL reverts?
validation_requirements: Simulate creditMultiplier at 0.01e18 with a large loan default
Calculate exact thresholds where loss exceeds total supply
Verify auction cannot complete when notifyPnL reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for or engineer multiple bad debt events to reduce creditMultiplier
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Open a large loan at the current (already reduced) creditMultiplier
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Default on the loan, triggering auction and eventual loss notification
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: notifyPnL reverts due to underflow, leaving loan/auction in limbo
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Significant capital to open large loans; patience for creditMultiplier reduction

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Loss processing mechanism permanently broken for affected loan
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Auction cannot settle; nAuctionsInProgress stuck; setAuctionHouse blocked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires governor emergencyAction to manually close the loan and adjust accounting
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All auction settlements, loss processing, creditMultiplier updates

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced - precise calculation of loss amplification
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After creditMultiplier drops below critical threshold

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: creditMultiplier approaching critical levels; large loans opened at low multiplier
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed bid() transactions with reverts in notifyPnL
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large borrowing during periods of low creditMultiplier

threats.title: ERC20Gauges._decrementWeightUntilFree Infinite Loop When User Has Zero-Weight Gauge Entry
threats.hypothesis_description: In ERC20Gauges._decrementWeightUntilFree(), the loop counter `i` is only incremented inside the `if (userGaugeWeight != 0)` block. If a user has a gauge in their set with 0 weight, the loop iterates infinitely on that gauge, consuming all gas. A 0-weight gauge entry can be created by calling incrementGauge(gauge, 0), which adds the gauge to _userGauges but leaves getUserGaugeWeight at 0. This blocks the user from transferring or burning any GUILD tokens that require weight freeing.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `unchecked { ++i; }` placement inside a conditional block is a classic off-by-one/loop-control bug. Any path that creates 0-weight gauge entries enables the DoS.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { ++i; } is inside if(userGaugeWeight != 0) block; if gauge has 0 weight, i never increments, infinite loop

preconditions: User has at least one gauge with 0 weight in their _userGauges set
User attempts to transfer/burn tokens requiring weight freeing (getUserWeight > balance - amount)
0-weight gauge appears in iteration before enough non-zero gauges are freed

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: incrementGauge(gauge, 0) successfully adds gauge to user set without adding weight; no require(weight > 0) check
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - self-inflicted or exploitable through SurplusGuildMinter if mintRatio is set to 0
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent once 0-weight gauge is in user's set

operational_questions: Can a user self-rescue by decrementing the 0-weight gauge?
Does SurplusGuildMinter.stake() with mintRatio=0 trigger this?
What are the realistic paths to creating 0-weight gauge entries?
validation_requirements: Call incrementGauge(validGauge, 0), then attempt to transfer tokens requiring weight freeing
Verify infinite loop / gas exhaustion
Test if decrementGauge(gauge, 0) can remove the entry

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Call incrementGauge(liveGauge, 0) to add 0-weight gauge entry to own set
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Allocate remaining balance to other gauges (getUserWeight near balance)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attempt transfer - _decrementWeightUntilFree hits infinite loop on 0-weight gauge
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Tokens permanently locked - cannot transfer, burn, or be used
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - self-inflicted DoS (primarily a code defect)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Affected user's GUILD tokens permanently locked; cannot participate in governance
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery path without protocol upgrade or emergencyAction
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: GuildToken transfers, burns, and weight management for affected users

threats.title: Profit Distributed to Zero-Weight Gauge Permanently Locks CREDIT in ProfitManager
threats.hypothesis_description: In ProfitManager.notifyPnL() when processing profit, the amountForGuild is computed and subtracted from the total before calculating amountForCredit. However, if the gauge's weight is 0 at the time of notification, gaugeProfitIndex is not updated (skipped because no voters to distribute to). The CREDIT tokens for the guild split remain on the ProfitManager contract with no claimant. This can occur when a gauge is deprecated between when a loan was called and when its auction settles.
threats.investigation_rationale: The profit split computes amountForGuild regardless of gauge weight, but only updates the distribution index if weight > 0. The difference is permanently lost.

proof_of_signal.pattern: amountForGuild computed and deducted from total; gaugeProfitIndex only updated if gaugeWeight != 0; if weight is 0, amountForGuild CREDIT stays on ProfitManager unclaimed

preconditions: A gauge is deprecated (weight removed from totals) via offboarding
An auction for a loan on that term settles after deprecation, calling notifyPnL with profit
guildSplit in profitSharingConfig is > 0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Profit split math subtracts amountForGuild from available credit but conditionally skips the distribution
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - natural occurrence during offboarding/auction sequence
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During the window between term deprecation and auction settlement

operational_questions: How large is the typical guildSplit portion of profits?
How often do auctions settle after the gauge has been deprecated?
Can the stuck CREDIT be recovered via surplus buffer withdrawal?
validation_requirements: Simulate: offboard a term with active auctions, let auctions settle with profit
Check ProfitManager CREDIT balance vs surplusBuffer + claimable rewards
Verify excess CREDIT is permanently stuck

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify terms being offboarded that have active loans
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for auctions to settle after term deprecation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: CREDIT from guild profit split is permanently stuck on ProfitManager
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Over time, cumulative stuck CREDIT reduces effective protocol solvency

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Gradual accumulation of stuck CREDIT; reduces effective surplus buffer capacity
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: May require governor emergencyAction to rescue stuck funds
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Profit distribution accuracy, ProfitManager solvency accounting

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ProfitManager CREDIT balance exceeding surplusBuffer + sum(termSurplusBuffer) + expected claimable

threats.title: LendingTerm.forgive() on Active Auction Creates Stuck Auction, Permanently Inflating nAuctionsInProgress
threats.hypothesis_description: LendingTerm.forgive() does not check if the loan is currently in an active auction (callTime != 0). If a governor calls forgive() on a loan that has been called and has an active auction, it sets closeTime and decrements issuance. Subsequently, any attempt to settle the auction via AuctionHouse.bid() or AuctionHouse.forgive() calls LendingTerm.onBid(), which reverts because `require(loans[loanId].closeTime == 0)`. The auction can never complete, and nAuctionsInProgress is permanently stuck above 0, blocking LendingTerm.setAuctionHouse().
threats.investigation_rationale: The loan state machine allows a direct Open/Called -> Closed transition via forgive() that bypasses the auction settlement path, creating an impossible auction state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: forgive() checks borrowTime != 0 and closeTime == 0 but NOT callTime == 0; line 556 sets closeTime; onBid at line 588 requires closeTime == 0

preconditions: A loan is in an active auction (callTime != 0, closeTime == 0)
Governor calls forgive() on that loanId
Governor role is required - not exploitable by unprivileged users

initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: GOVERNOR role required - this is an admin operation bug, not an external attack
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: While a loan is in active auction

operational_questions: Can governor recover from this via emergencyAction on AuctionHouse?
Does the double issuance decrement cause further accounting issues?
Is forgive() during auction a documented emergency procedure?
validation_requirements: Test: call loan -> forgive loan -> attempt to bid on auction
Verify nAuctionsInProgress cannot be decremented
Verify setAuctionHouse is permanently blocked

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised or mistaken governor calls forgive() on a loan with active auction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Auction enters irrecoverable state - onBid reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: setAuctionHouse blocked, requiring emergencyAction to fix

threats.title: ProfitSharingConfig Truncation Causes Persistent Rounding Imbalance in Profit Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: ProfitManager.setProfitSharingConfig() validates that splits sum to 1e18 at 18-decimal precision, but stores them as uint32 values divided by 1e9 (9-decimal precision). Each stored value loses up to (1e9 - 1) precision. When read back via getProfitSharingConfig(), the creditSplit is computed as `1e18 - surplusBufferSplit*1e9 - guildSplit*1e9 - otherSplit*1e9`. The truncation means creditSplit absorbs up to 3*(1e9-1) ≈ 3e9 extra wei per distribution. Over many distributions, this systematically redirects funds from surplus buffer/guild/other to credit holders.
threats.investigation_rationale: The precision loss is structural and occurs every time profit is distributed. While small per-transaction, it compounds across the lifetime of the protocol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint32(surplusBufferSplit / 1e9) truncates; each split loses up to 1e9-1; creditSplit = 1e18 - sum absorbs rounding

preconditions: profitSharingConfig is set with values not perfectly divisible by 1e9
Frequent profit distributions occur

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Input validated at 18 decimals, stored at 9 decimals, read back at 18 decimals with residual going to creditSplit
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Governor must set the config - the truncation is inherent in any non-aligned values

operational_questions: What is the maximum per-distribution deviation?
Over expected protocol lifetime, what is the cumulative misallocation?
Are there governance-set values that align perfectly to 1e9?
validation_requirements: Set splits to non-aligned values and compute actual vs expected distribution
Quantify cumulative drift over 1000 distributions
Verify creditSplit absorbs all truncation errors

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Calculate truncation per split (up to 1e9-1 each)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Each distribution slightly favors credit holders over surplus buffer/guild/other
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Cumulative effect reduces surplus buffer growth rate

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Slight systematic misallocation of profits away from surplus buffer
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot be fixed without code change to storage precision

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Compare emitted ProfitSharingConfigUpdate values with actual distribution splits
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Surplus buffer growing slower than expected from interest revenue

threats.title: ERC20RebaseDistributor updateTotalRebasingShares Overflow Can Trap Last Rebasing Users' Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: In ERC20RebaseDistributor.updateTotalRebasingShares(), when shares decrease significantly (most rebasing supply exits), the adjusted target share price is calculated as: `targetNewSharePrice = currentRebasingSharePrice + (delta * START_REBASING_SHARE_PRICE) / percentChange`. When `percentChange` is very small (close to 0, meaning sharesAfter << sharesBefore), the division amplifies the result, potentially exceeding uint224 max (2^224 - 1 ≈ 2.7e67). The safeCastTo224 call would then revert, blocking _exitRebase() for remaining rebasing users, permanently trapping their CREDIT tokens in the rebasing mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The share price adjustment inversely scales with the ratio of remaining to previous shares. Extreme share exits (e.g., 99.999% of rebasing supply leaving) can create values that overflow uint224.

proof_of_signal.pattern: targetNewSharePrice = currentRebasingSharePrice + (delta * START_REBASING_SHARE_PRICE) / percentChange; percentChange can be near-zero when most shares exit; safeCastTo224 can revert

preconditions: Large share price delta exists (distribution in progress, not fully interpolated)
Most rebasing supply exits in a single transaction or sequence
sharesAfter is extremely small compared to sharesBefore
The resulting targetNewSharePrice exceeds uint224 max

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Division by (sharesAfter * 1e30 / sharesBefore) where sharesAfter approaches 0 causes amplification toward overflow
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - can occur during normal exitRebase/transfer operations
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During distribution interpolation when most rebasing supply exits

operational_questions: What is the minimum sharesAfter/sharesBefore ratio that causes overflow?
Can an attacker manipulate rebasing supply to engineer this condition?
What happens to the last rebaser's funds if exitRebase reverts?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact threshold where targetNewSharePrice overflows uint224
Test with a large distribution followed by mass exit leaving 1 wei of shares
Verify exitRebase reverts for remaining users

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Trigger a large distribute() to create significant share price delta
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Exit most rebasing supply (or engineer mass exit via large transfers)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Remaining rebasing users' exitRebase reverts due to uint224 overflow
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victims' CREDIT tokens permanently locked in rebasing state
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires controlling majority of rebasing supply

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Last rebasing users permanently locked; cannot transfer, burn, or exit rebase
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery without code upgrade - forceExitRebase would also call _exitRebase and revert
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: CreditToken transfers, burns, exitRebase for trapped users

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Large CREDIT holdings to control rebasing supply
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: High - need to control majority of rebasing supply
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced - precise calculation of overflow conditions

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large exitRebase events leaving very small remaining rebasing supply
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed exitRebase transactions with SafeCast overflow
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Coordinated mass exit from rebasing during distribution period
